title: The Arsenal of Middle Powers
subhead: As great-power competition fractures traditional security architecture, second-tier states are building indigenous tech stacks and networked weapons systems — not to challenge the hegemon, but to survive the squeeze between them.

sections.heading: NATO's Counter-Drone Marketplace: Pre-Vetted Dependence
sections.body:
NATO is establishing a counter-drone marketplace that will select 18 C-UAS systems by summer 2026, offering member nations pre-vetted solutions for point, area, and border defense. The alliance will test systems at standardized "innovation ranges" in Latvia and elsewhere, awarding "innovation badges" that certify performance against NATO criteria. Nations can then procure immediately through pre-completed contracts — or lease systems for testing before commitment.

The structural shift isn't the technology — it's the procurement architecture. NATO is moving from requirement-based acquisition (nations specify, industry builds) to challenge-based procurement (industry pitches, NATO vets, nations select from a menu). This compresses acquisition timelines from years to weeks, crucial when Ukrainian drone tactics evolve every two to three weeks according to NATO officials.

The trap: standardized solutions create standardized vulnerabilities. Every nation that adopts the same C-UAS stack creates a single point of failure. The adversary who defeats one system defeats them all. NATO's efficiency gain is also a homogenization risk — and homogenized defenses are easier to probe than heterogeneous ones.
sections.whyItMatters: NATO's marketplace model trades sovereignty for speed. Nations that outsource their counter-drone stack to alliance-vetted solutions gain rapid capability but lose the institutional knowledge that comes from building indigenous systems. Turkey — notably operating outside this framework — has chosen the harder path of domestic development, and now exports the results.

sections.heading: Turkey's Networked Arsenal: The Commercial-Component Strategy
sections.body:
At SAHA Expo 2026 in Istanbul, Turkish defense giant Aselsan unveiled a new generation of autonomous weapons designed around a deliberately commercial philosophy: Tufan unmanned surface vessels for swarming strike operations, Kılıç underwater drones carrying torpedo-class warheads, and the Koral AD electronic warfare system for the layered "Steel Dome" air defense architecture. CEO Ahmet Akyol stated the systems could be in inventory by 2027, designed for mass production rather than boutique defense industry approaches.

Turkey has cracked what middle powers across the Global South are attempting: a defense industrial base that produces networked, autonomous systems at commercial scale using commercial supply chains. The Kılıç UUV variants (10 and 200) are described as "kamikaze" platforms that can swarm autonomously or via satellite link — low-signature, long-range, high-volume. Aselsan's export target: 40% of revenues by 2030, up from 25% today.

The decisive move: Turkey isn't trying to compete with US or Chinese high-end systems. It's building the mid-tier — capable enough for most conflicts, cheap enough for mass procurement, unencumbered by Western export restrictions. The trap for Turkey's competitors is underestimating how many nations want exactly that: systems that work, at prices they can afford, without political strings.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's model demonstrates that technology sovereignty isn't about matching great-power capabilities across the stack. It's about controlling a specific layer — in this case, networked autonomous weapons designed for attritional warfare — and exporting that capability to states excluded from Western defense markets. The Global South's armed forces don't need F-35s. They need drones that work.

sections.heading: Canada-Turkey Defense Tie: Middle Powers Organize Outside US Architecture
sections.body:
Canada's Secretary of State for Defense Procurement Stephen Fuhr visited SAHA Expo 2026 seeking partnership with Turkey on drones, counter-drones, and ammunition production. "The middle powers have to come together in a way they didn't before," Fuhr told Defense News. "Individually we're just not big enough, but collectively we are." The visit comes as Ottawa seeks trusted partners amid "shifting global security dynamics" — code for uncertainty about US reliability.

A NATO member is publicly building defense industrial ties with another NATO member specifically because the traditional US-led security architecture can no longer be taken for granted. Canada, which imposed an arms embargo on Turkey from 2019 to 2024, now sees Turkish drones and autonomous systems as essential to its own capability gaps. Fuhr compared Turkey's defense industrial progress to South Korea and France — mature, independent ecosystems.

The trap for Ottawa: partnering with Turkey means accepting that Turkish systems come with Turkish political interests. The same country selling you drones may be operating them against Kurdish forces, or in conflicts where Canada has taken opposing positions. Middle-power solidarity has its limits when partners have divergent strategic interests.
sections.whyItMatters: Canada's overture signals a broader recognition: the old model of middle powers buying American and trusting US security guarantees is fraying. States that once outsourced their defense industrial base to the US are now diversifying — not away from NATO, but into bilateral arrangements that hedge against US unpredictability. The network is replacing the hierarchy.

sections.heading: US Greenland Base Expansion: Sovereign Enclaves in the GIUK Gap
sections.body:
The US is negotiating with Denmark to open three new military bases in southern Greenland, with officials floating an arrangement where the facilities would be designated as US sovereign territory. The bases would focus on surveillance of the GIUK Gap — the chokepoint between Greenland, Iceland, and the UK where Russian and Chinese maritime activity transits between the Arctic and Atlantic. Negotiations have progressed quietly despite President Trump's January threat to seize Greenland "the easy way or the hard way."

The 1951 US-Denmark defense pact already grants Washington broad rights to expand in Greenland, and Copenhagen has never rejected a US request for military expansion there. The proposed Narsarsuaq base would reactivate a former US facility. But the twist is the sovereignty framing: US officials are pushing for the bases to be formally designated US territory — not just leased facilities. This would create permanent American enclaves on Danish soil, controlling the Arctic gateway.

The trap: bases designated as sovereign territory are harder to close than leased facilities. Denmark may be calculating that granting sovereign bases is preferable to the alternative Trump threatened — seizure. But the precedent is corrosive. If the US can extract territorial concessions through threats, every ally with strategic real estate faces the same pressure.
sections.whyItMatters: The Greenland negotiations reveal the gap between Trump's rhetoric and his administration's execution. The threat of seizure was the coercive lever; the negotiated bases are the actual objective. The outcome — three new US-controlled surveillance posts in the Arctic — is a significant capability gain. The cost is the demonstration that allies can be extorted, and that NATO solidarity has limits when a member state threatens another's territorial integrity.

sections.heading: China's EV Export Surge: Industrial Overcapacity as Geopolitical Lever
sections.body:
China's passenger car exports jumped 85% year-on-year in April to 796,000 vehicles, with new energy vehicle exports up 120% to 420,000 units. Meanwhile, domestic sales fell 25.5% — the sixth straight month of decline. The driver: weakened government subsidies and a prolonged property sector downturn have crushed domestic demand, forcing Chinese automakers to export or die. BYD is building factories in Europe and Latin America; Chinese EVs captured one in six Australian sales in April.

China's EV export boom isn't primarily a trade strategy — it's an industrial overcapacity problem seeking foreign markets because domestic absorption has collapsed. The property crisis that has gutted Chinese consumer spending has created a massive supply-demand imbalance in autos. Beijing's choice: let factories idle and workers lose jobs, or dump production abroad. The 100% US tariff imposed by Biden in 2024 has effectively blocked the American market; Europe remains partially open despite ongoing trade disputes.

The trap for Beijing: export dependence on markets that can be closed by tariff policy makes Chinese industry vulnerable to political decisions in Brussels, Ottawa, and Canberra. The trap for importing nations: cheap Chinese EVs accelerate the transition away from oil (beneficial as Hormuz disruption drives petrol prices to $4.50/gallon), but they also hollow out domestic auto industries and create dependencies on Chinese battery supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: China's EV surge demonstrates how industrial policy and geopolitical competition collide. Beijing spent a decade building EV capacity that now exceeds domestic demand by a wide margin. The surplus must go somewhere. Importing nations face a choice: accept cheap EVs and the supply-chain dependency they create, or protect domestic industries and slow their energy transition. There is no clean option.

sections.heading: California Mayor as Chinese Agent: Local Influence, Strategic Access
sections.body:
Eileen Wang, the mayor of Arcadia, California (population 53,000, majority Asian), has agreed to plead guilty to acting as an illegal agent of the Chinese government. From late 2020 to 2022, Wang and a colleague operated the "U.S. News Center" website targeting Chinese-American communities, posting pro-PRC content at Beijing's direction — including articles denying Uyghur genocide. Wang's colleague, Yaoning "Mike" Sun, is already serving a four-year sentence; Wang faces up to 10 years.

The case illustrates China's influence strategy at the municipal level: not targeting classified information or federal policy, but shaping local Chinese-language media narratives and cultivating relationships with local officials who can later ascend to state or federal positions. Arcadia isn't a strategic target for its own sake — it's a practice ground. Wang was elected in 2022; had she continued rising, she would have carried pro-Beijing sympathies into higher office.

The trap for Beijing: exposure carries costs. Each prosecuted case hardens federal scrutiny of Chinese-American political activity and fuels narratives about Chinese infiltration. The trap for Washington: overreaction risks alienating Chinese-American communities whose civic participation is both legitimate and valuable. The balance between vigilance and discrimination is politically contested and operationally difficult.
sections.whyItMatters: Influence operations targeting local officials represent a long-game strategy: cultivate relationships with rising politicians before they reach positions where they can affect national policy. The Arcadia case is one node in a larger network. The structural question is how many similar operations remain undetected — and how the US security apparatus adapts to a threat that operates below the threshold of traditional counterintelligence.

sections.heading: Sweden Sanctions Bust: Western Tech to Russia's War Machine
sections.body:
Swedish authorities detained two suspects — a Swedish national and a Turkish national — for allegedly facilitating "advanced products" shipments to Russia in violation of EU sanctions. The technology had potential military applications in Ukraine. The arrests follow Sweden's recent detention of two suspected Russian shadow fleet tankers in the Baltic Sea, part of a broader crackdown on sanctions evasion schemes.

Sanctions regimes are only as strong as their weakest enforcement point. Despite EU restrictions, Russian procurement networks continue sourcing Western technology through intermediaries in neutral or permissive jurisdictions. The Turkish national arrested in Sweden illustrates the challenge: Turkey, a NATO member, has not joined EU sanctions against Russia and remains a potential conduit for dual-use goods. The six-year maximum sentence if convicted signals Swedish resolve, but prosecutions are reactive — the technology has often already reached its destination.

The decisive move for Russia: build redundant procurement networks through countries that won't enforce Western sanctions. The decisive move for the West: target the intermediaries and shipping infrastructure that make evasion possible. Sweden's shadow fleet detentions and sanctions-busting arrests represent the latter approach. But the structural reality remains: Russia continues to source Western chips for its missiles, and enforcement will always lag behind procurement innovation.
sections.whyItMatters: Sanctions evasion is an industry, and industries adapt. The Swedish case demonstrates that enforcement is possible but resource-intensive. The strategic question is whether Western governments can sustain enforcement attention across multiple jurisdictions — Turkey, Central Asia, the Gulf — or whether sanctions will gradually leak into irrelevance as evasion networks mature.

bottomLine: The technology-power landscape of May 2026 is defined by middle-power agency. Turkey builds and exports autonomous weapons to states excluded from Western markets. Canada seeks Turkish partnership because American reliability cannot be assumed. China floods global markets with EVs because domestic demand collapsed. The unifying thread: second-tier states are no longer passive consumers of great-power technology. They are building indigenous capabilities, forming bilateral partnerships outside alliance structures, and exploiting gaps in the US-led order. The Arsenal of Middle Powers is loading — and its customers are everywhere the great powers have neglected.
sources: Defense News, BBC, AP News, Kyiv Independent, Washington Post, SCMP, Reuters, Swedish Security Service, Texas Attorney General, NATO, Aselsan, China Association of Automobile Manufacturers
